Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ha, Meem. (1)

Like the preceding Surahs, the Qur'an name of this Surah is also 'Ha-Meem'. The sharing of names among the Surahs, as we have pointed out, is proof of the sharing of their contents. Thus, if you reflect, it emerges that in this Surah, there is the detail of that warning which was given to the Quraysh in the final verse of the preceding Surah regarding the end of denying the messenger. The subsequent verses will make this entirely clear.

"By the clear Book," (2)

The Qur'an is Itself a Proof of Its Every Claim

'Wa' (By) here is used for an oath, and we have explained in its proper place that the primary objective of an oath in Arabic is to present a witness to a promise. For this reason, the question arises: what is the statement sworn to (the maqsam 'alayh)? Generally, our commentators have declared the subsequent verse: 'Indeed, We sent it down during a blessed night...' as the statement sworn to. Although in terms of the rules of the language there is no deficiency in this, I am not fully satisfied with it. The relation between the oath and the statement sworn to is of proof and claim. Here, it is difficult to understand how the Qur'an's being a clear Book can be a proof of the fact that Allah Almighty sent it down during a blessed night. In my view, the statement sworn to is omitted here. The context and the flow of the discourse are proofs of this omission. There are several parallels of omitting the statement sworn to due to contextual clarity. An obvious parallel is present in Surah Qaf: 'Qaf. By the honored Qur'an... But they wonder that there has come to them a warner from among themselves...' (1-2); obviously, the statement sworn to is omitted there. Similarly, in the verse under discussion, the statement sworn to is omitted. The benefit of this omission is that all those matters can be assumed omitted which the context of the discourse requires. If this omission is spelled out, the complete statement is as if: 'This clear Book, which is itself a decisive proof of its claims, is witness to the fact that whatever bad end of which it is warning the deniers is bound to happen; the person presenting it is not crazy or mad, but a clear messenger. His invitation is based entirely on wisdom. Accepting it is a source of mercy, and rejecting it is a source of punishment.' The meaning is: to find proof for these realities, there is no need to go anywhere outside the Qur'an. This Book is itself sufficient to prove every reality. Those who are denying it are inviting their own ruin.

"Indeed, We sent it down during a blessed night. Indeed, We were warning." (3)

This describes that special care which Allah Almighty showed to send down this Book. The objective of mentioning this care is to clarify to the addressees that they should not consider this a joke or the whispering of a crazy person; rather, this is a highly great part of a highly great plan of Allah Almighty, and to send it down, Allah Almighty chose that blessed night which is specified for the division of all wise matters from Him. The objective of its sending down is to warn those whose ancestors were not warned, so that the proof of Allah is fully established against them and they cannot present the excuse on the Day of Judgment that they were seized without warning. Some other aspects of this special care will, God willing, follow in the commentaries of Surah Al-Jinn and Surah Al-Qadr.

By 'blessed night' (laylatun mubarakah), obviously, Laylat-ul-Qadr (the Night of Decree) is meant. Thus, this is explicitly stated in Surah Al-Qadr: 'Indeed, We sent it [i.e., the Qur'an] down during the Night of Decree. And what can make you know what is the Night of Decree? The Night of Decree is better than a thousand months. The angels and the Spirit descend therein by permission of their Lord for every matter. Peace it is until the emergence of dawn.' (1-5).

This Laylat-ul-Qadr is necessarily some night of Ramadan, because it is explicitly stated in the Qur'an that the Qur'an was revealed in the month of Ramadan: 'The month of Ramadhan [is that] in which was revealed the Qur'an...' (Al-Baqarah: 185).

As for the question: which night of Ramadan is this? It is difficult to give a decisive answer. In the light of traditions, it can only be said that it is some night of the final ten days of Ramadan. The wisdom in not specifying it is to make the servants search for it, and thus test their passion, desire, and quest. The secret of all the blessings of Laylat-ul-Qadr is hidden within this very quest and desire of the servants.

From these explanations, it emerged automatically that there is no room to interpret this as any night of Sha'ban or any other month.

By sending down the Qur'an in this night, it is not required that the entire Qur'an was sent down in this single night; rather, this is to inform of its blessed beginning. When the beginning of a task occurred in a blessed hour and the decision of its completion was also made, it is as if the task was completed in that very blessed hour, because no one can alter the decisions of Allah Almighty. There are examples of this style of expression in the Qur'an showing that Allah Almighty has described promises to be fulfilled in the future using the past tense.

The indefiniteness (tankeer) of 'laylatun mubarakah' is to magnify its status. The blessing of specific days, months, and times is in terms of their spiritual fertility and favor. Just as in our physical world, seasons and harvests are considered, and every season is not suitable for cultivating everything, similarly, in the spiritual world, times, hours, years, and months are considered. The blessing and reality of that worship which Allah has associated with a specific time, day, or month is manifest only when it is performed with the discipline of that time, day, or month. Otherwise, just as wheat sowed out of season remains without harvest, similarly, prayer out of time, fasting out of time, and Hajj out of season are of no use, or of very little use. Among our twenty-four hours, the times of Fajr, Chasht, Zuhr, Asr, Maghrib, Isha, and Tahajjud possess a specific blessing in spiritual terms. Their blessing has been clarified in the Qur'an and Hadiths. Similarly, among the days of the week, the day of Friday holds a specific significance in terms of spiritual favor which no other day holds... among the twelve months of the year, the months of Ramadan and Hajj hold a specific honor in which other months do not share. By the same token, among the nights of the final ten days of Ramadan, Allah Almighty has specified a single night in which He, through His close angels, informs the angels appointed in this world of His plan for the year so that they execute His command at its specified time. In a similar night, the Lord of Honor sent down the Qur'an so that people were warned through it, and the mercy destined in the form of sending down a final messenger was manifest.

By referring to this, it is intended to clarify the reality that the sending down of this Qur'an is neither an accident, nor a tune out of time, nor a wild plant out of season, nor a self-made thing; but this is the manifestation of that plan which Allah Almighty chose for the reform and guidance of His creation. Thus, He sent it down in this blessed night which is specified for the division of all wise matters. Therefore, those for whom it has been sent down must value it in accordance with its majesty; otherwise, they should remember that He will never forgive the disregard of what He has sent down with this care and arrangement. This is no ordinary thing which they can brush off in jest. Its acceptance and denial are both of highly important consequences, and these consequences are bound to appear.

Eternal Kingdom or Eternal Ruin

'Indeed, We were warning' (Inna kunna mundhireen)—this describes the objective of the sending down—stating that in the plan of Allah, it has been decided that just as previous nations were fully informed of good and evil before being destroyed for their disobedience, similarly, the Arabs should be fully informed before being punished for their disobedience, so that those who wish to accept guidance do so, and those who do not, are left with no excuse. The meaning is: the sending down of this Qur'an and this messengership is for the final establishment of proof from Allah, and His Sunnah is that after the establishment of proof, no nation receives respite; therefore, everyone should choose their path after reflecting on the decisive stage before them. They have to choose between two things: the eternal kingdom or the eternal ruin!

"Therein is distinct every wise matter," (4)

A Warning to the Addressees

This is the description of this blessed night—stating that in it is divided every wise matter. If you look at this verse in the light of Surah Al-Qadr, it emerges that in this night, Allah Almighty informs His close angels of all those general matters that relate to the earth, and they use them to inform the angels appointed in the earth, in accordance with the design appointed by Allah.

With 'matter' (amr), the attribute 'wise' (hakeem) is to show the reality that no command of Allah is devoid of wisdom. He decides to send down punishment upon any nation, and this is based on His justice and wisdom; and He sends His mercy upon any nation, and this is based on His justice and wisdom. This is a warning to the addressees: reflect on every aspect of the stage before you with seriousness. If you adopt a negligent attitude and do not fulfill the requirements of a wise plan of God, its results will be highly fatal for you.

"[As] command from Us. Indeed, We were sending [messengers]," (5)

The accusative case (nasb) of 'Amran' (command) is for special specification, and is meant to clarify the importance and greatness of this division of matters—showing that whatever happens, happens by the specific command of God. No one else has any share in it; therefore, the servants must accept it as the specific command of the true King of the universe, and obey His every command with a sincere spirit of submission. If they reject it, mock it, and deny it, they should remember that this will be rebellion against the true King of the universe, the punishment for which is highly terrifying.

Pointing to the Prophecies of the Prophets

'Indeed, We were sending [messengers]'—this is a phrase of the same weight as 'Indeed, We were warning' above. Just as in the preceding verse, the objective of the Qur'an's sending down was stated as warning, in this verse, the reality is clarified: in the plan of Allah, it was decided from before that He would send a messenger among the Children of Ismail who would be a mercy for them and for the entire creation. This points to those prophecies of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), and Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) which have passed. The meaning is: this messenger's advent has occurred under that very plan, and has occurred precisely in that night which is appointed by Allah Almighty for the manifestation of such important matters.

"[As] mercy from your Lord. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing." (6)

Pointing to the Prophet's (pbuh) Being a Mercy

This describes the objective of sending the messenger, and the address is to the Prophet (pbuh)—stating that your Lord has sent you as a great mercy from Himself. If people do not recognize your worth, they will harm you in no way, but will deprive themselves of the greatest mercy of God. In verse 3, the messenger's being a warner was mentioned; in this verse, His being a mercy and glad tidings is pointed to. And both of these are two aspects of a single reality: what is the greatest mercy can also become the greatest punishment if its worth is not recognized.

It should be kept in mind here that the aspect of warning was mentioned first, whereas the primary objective of sending down the Qur'an and sending the messenger is mercy to creation. The reason is what we pointed to when discussing the core theme of the Surah—stating that the actual character of this Surah is warning.

The Necessary Requirement of Allah's Hearing and Knowing

'Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing'—within this is kept in view that the Lord of this universe is a Knowing, Seeing Being; He cannot leave His creation without purpose. His being Knowing and Seeing requires that He keeps watch over the states of His creation, and informs them of His commands. If they comply with them, He rewards them in both this world and the Hereafter, and if they rebel, He punishes them. Second, what the leaders of the Quraysh are doing with the Book of Allah and His messenger today is in the knowledge of the Hearing, Knowing God. Every matter is in His knowledge, and since every matter is in His knowledge and nothing is outside His power, the Prophet and the believers should remain assured: whatever His wisdom requires will necessarily stand manifest. Nothing will obstruct His way.

To understand these verses, have a look at what we wrote under verses 45-46 of Surah Al-Qasas.

"Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, if you should be certain." (7)

A Severe Warning to the Opponents

This is a warning, and a highly severe warning to the opponents. It states: do not consider this warning and glad tidings as an empty threat which is said merely to overawe you; rather, this is from the Lord of the heavens and the earth and all that is between them. The meaning is: when He alone is the Owner and Lord of everything, who is capable of obstructing His will? If He wishes to seize people, He can seize them whenever He pleases, and no one can save them; and if He wishes to bless anyone with His favor, He can bless them as He pleases, and no one can snatch it.

'if you should be certain' (in kuntum mooqineen)—this is a reproach to the addressees: indeed this is a self-evident reality, everyone should accept it, but to accept something, it is not sufficient that it is clear and self-evident; rather, it is also necessary that the desire to accept is present within a person. If this desire is absent, he will deny even the most obvious reality, and no logician will be capable of convincing him.

"There is no deity except Him; He gives life and causes death. [He is] your Lord and the Lord of your first forefathers." (8)

This is a further emphasis of the preceding statement—showing that there is no deity besides Him whose intercession can be of any use to you. Life and death are entirely in His control. He is your Lord and the Lord of your ancestors. If your ancestors worshipped others besides Him, this was their foolishness and ignorance. Do not present their blind following as proof for yourselves, otherwise you will lose your way.

"But they are in doubt, amusing themselves." (9)

The Actual Cause of Denial

That is, this warning and glad tidings is a real event, in which there is no room for doubt for any wise person, but these people are such slaves of their desires that until they see the very thing of which they are being warned with their eyes, they are not going to believe; because of this, they are caught in doubt, playing and making fun of it. This consoled the Prophet (pbuh)—showing that convincing those whose approach to the affairs of life is negligent is not your job. These people will believe when they see the whip of punishment; so leave them to their state.

"Then watch for the Day when the sky will bring a visible smoke. Enveloping the people; this is a painful punishment." (10-11)

Await for them that Day when the sky will appear with a visible smoke which will envelop everyone, proclaiming through its language of state: 'This is indeed that painful punishment.' This is that threat of punishment which they were demanding, and before whose seeing they were not ready to accept the Prophet's warning.

What is Meant by 'a visible smoke'?

Here, the question arises: what is meant by 'a visible smoke' (dukhanun mubeen)? 'Dukhan' means smoke, and the attribute 'mubeen' clearly means it will be prominent and visible to everyone. No room for doubt will remain for anyone in it.

One group of commentators has answered that this smoke will appear at the time of the Resurrection. In support, they have also quoted a tradition, but critics of Hadith have declared this tradition to be of the storytellers and criticized it. In our view, this tradition is also contrary to the context of the verses. Below you will see that the context clearly shows that this is the mention of some punishment which came upon nations as a result of denying the messengers, whose details have passed in the stories of 'Aad, Thamud, the people of Shu'ayb, etc.

Another group has interpreted this as a famine which, according to their statement, came upon the Quraysh after migration as a result of the Prophet's (pbuh) supplication against them, which became so severe that people were forced to eat carcasses, and due to hunger, every person saw the sky in the form of smoke.

Generally, our commentators have adopted this second view, but I find some matters in it which are hard to accept.

First, the mention of such a supplication of the Prophet against his entire nation is found only in this commentarial tradition. Other than this, there is no other testimony that the Prophet (pbuh) ever supplicated against his nation. His supplication for his nation was always: 'O my Lord, guide my people because they do not know.' The most critical moment was of migration. At that moment, some other messengers supplicated against their nations, but no word of supplication against his nation came out of the Prophet's (pbuh) mouth. You stated only: 'O Makkah, you are highly beloved to me, but what can I do, your sons do not let me live here.' During various battles, when the armies of the Quraysh and the Muslims stood face to face, the supplications you made were for the steadfastness of the believers and the victory of the truth. Any word against the enemies was no more than asking Allah to cast terror into their hearts and shake their feet. I do not recall that even on those occasions you supplicated against your entire nation.

Second, after migration, the most critical moment was of Hudaybiyyah, when the ignorance and bias of the Quraysh appeared openly, and the emotions of the Muslims were highly incited against them. But even on that occasion, you (pbuh) did not supplicate against the Quraysh. Not only did you not supplicate against them, but you suppressed the incited emotions of the Muslims and prevented them from battle; and the Qur'an, as will follow in the commentary of Surah Al-Fath, clarified the wisdom of preventing this battle—showing that if battle had occurred, those who believed in their hearts but could not migrate due to their difficulties would have suffered harm. Reflect: when for the sake of these hidden Muslims of Makkah, the Muslims put back their drawn swords into their scabbards, and the Prophet (pbuh) made a treaty with the Quraysh which was contrary to the general emotions of the companions, how could you supplicate against these people of Makkah for such a famine in which people were forced to eat carcasses? If any such famine had occurred, its target would have been the poor and common people, among whom were many Muslims, not the chiefs of Makkah and Ta'if.

Third, the Prophet (pbuh) was neither commanded to supplicate nor to supplicate against them, but was instructed to observe patience and wait for a Day when the sky will appear with such a smoke which will envelop the entire nation, proclaiming through its language of state: 'This is indeed that painful punishment' of which people were warned but which they mocked. As if its nature is of a warning, and warning is conditional on circumstances. In the Qur'an, the Quraysh were repeatedly warned of a punishment of the kind that came upon 'Aad, Thamud, the people of Lut, and the people of Shu'ayb, but no such punishment came upon them. The reason is that the majority of the Arabs believed. Only a few wicked ones persisted on their mischief, who either ended in conflict with the believers or surrendered on the Conquest of Makkah.

Fourth, describing famine as 'a visible smoke' is not a suitable description. The theme of famine is a well-known theme of Arabic poetry. During the severe cold winds from the north, a state of famine arose in the country. In some regions, conditions became highly severe. Arab poets drew highly effective pictures of these conditions using metaphors and comparisons, but I do not recall that any poet ever described even the most severe famine as 'a visible smoke', or used this style of expression to describe its effect.

For these reasons, the tradition of famine, even if correct, does not relate to this verse. It is possible that some famine occurred, and it is also possible that this famine became highly severe; it has been the Sunnah of Allah that during the advent of the messengers, their nations were tested with such trials to warn them and turn them to Him. The indications of this Sunnah are present in the Qur'an. Despite all these possibilities, this verse does not seem to relate to any such famine whose intensity made everyone see the sky in the form of smoke.

By 'a visible smoke' (dukhanun mubeen), the mind goes not to famine, but to the punishment of a storm of stones and wind (hasib). This was the primary punishment that came upon previous nations of the Arabs as a retribution for denying the messengers. In the stories of 'Aad, Thamud, the people of Lut, and the people of Shu'ayb which have passed, the details of this punishment have been described. The picture of this punishment that emerges from the poetry of the Arabs and from the Qur'an is highly similar to the description 'a visible smoke'. Its form is that a column of black dust appears rising to the sky. In this dust, until the sun is completely hidden, its rays are also mixed, making observers feel as if a fire has broken out in some forest, whose smoke is rising to the sky. Then, when the intensity of the wind increases and this storm directs to some side, it appears that a black cloud is spread, which is about to rain. Then it takes a terrifying form, and covers the settlements with a rain of sand and stones. When the punishment came upon 'Aad, they believed the column of black dust to be a cloud. Thus, in Surah Al-Ahqaf, their mention is:

'And when they saw it as a cloud approaching their valleys, they said, "This is a cloud bringing us rain." Rather, it is that for which you were impatient: a wind, within it a painful punishment,' (24).

This same punishment, in the description of the destruction of the people of Shu'ayb, was expressed as 'the punishment of the Day of the Canopy' (adhabu Yawm-il-Dhullah):

'And they denied him, so the punishment of the Day of the Canopy seized them...' (Al-Shu'ara': 189). 'Dhullah' is used for a canopy, and also for a cloud.

The punishment of a storm of stones, in its initial stage, appears in the form of a rising cloud or smoke. For this reason, the plausible view is that this is the threat of that very punishment. This threat was given to the Quraysh in highly clear terms by narrating the stories of 'Aad, Thamud, etc. We have explained the nature of these nations' punishment in detail in the previous Surahs. These details will also follow in the subsequent Surahs. There, we will also present the mentor-scholar's (ra) research in this regard.

This threat was, as we pointed out above, conditional upon the opponents' denying the messenger, but the majority of the Quraysh accepted Islam, and after the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, the power of the opponents was completely broken, until on the Conquest of Makkah, the prophecy of entering the religion of Allah in groups (An-Nasr: 2) was fulfilled in such a way that everyone observed its truth. Obviously, for the threat which was conditional upon denial, no cause remained for its manifestation after this acceptance; therefore, the Quraysh as a whole remained safe from Allah's punishment. Only those wicked ones fell under the swords of the Muslims who attacked them, and those who survived surrendered on the Conquest of Makkah.

"Enveloping the people; this is a painful punishment." (11)

This describes the intensity of that punishment—showing that it will envelop people in such a way that no escape will remain for anyone. 'this is a painful punishment' is an expression through the language of state or the situation—showing that it will become clear to everyone that this is no temporary wind, but the wrath of God which will destroy everyone.

"[They will say], 'Our Lord, remove from us the punishment; indeed, we are believers.'" (12)

The State of the Arrogant after Observing the Punishment

That is, today they are showing great arrogance and demanding the punishment with great pomp, but when they fall under its grasp, they will pray: 'O our Lord! Remove this punishment from us. Now we believe.'

"How will there be for them a reminder [i.e., acceptance of faith] when there had already come to them a clear Messenger? Then they turned away from him and said, '[He is] a tutored madman.'" (13-14)

This is the reply that will be given to those people on behalf of Allah Almighty at that time. It states: after the punishment has come, where will there remain any opportunity for accepting the reminder? particularly when, to fully establish proof against them, Allah had also sent His messenger who explained every matter clearly, but they turned away from him with great arrogance, claiming: 'He is tutored by others, and has got a madness of the Resurrection.' Now the time for repentance has passed; the time for repentance was when the messenger was inviting to repentance. They lost that time, so now it is not going to return.

A reply of this type is repeatedly quoted for the arrogant caught in the grasp of the punishment. To some, it was said directly. For example, to Pharaoh: 'Now? And you had disobeyed before' (Yunus: 91). In another place:

'And warn, [O Muhammad], the people of a Day when the punishment will come to them and those who did wrong will say, "Our Lord, delay us for a short term; we will answer Your call and follow the messengers." [But it will be said], "Had you not sworn, before, that for you there would be no decline?"' (Ibrahim: 44).

In the verse under discussion, this is said turning away from them in the third-person style. Changes of style are according to the requirements of eloquence, and are not hidden from those of taste. In the second-person style, intensity is prominent, and in the third-person style, aversion.

A Slander Devised by the Quraysh to Turn People away from the Qur'an and the Prophet

The Quraysh's accusation against the Prophet (pbuh)—that other people teach you and you present these tutored statements to us claiming them to be divine revelation—is also mentioned in other places. This accusation was devised by the Quraysh specifically to satisfy those who, being influenced by the reasoning and contents of the Qur'an, began to think that an unlettered person could not present such a wise discourse without divine guidance. To turn such people away from the Qur'an, the Quraysh devised this slander: 'This discourse is not the fruit of any revelation, but the result of the conspiracy of some educated people. They devise this discourse secretly, and this person presents it as revelation. The objective of the conspirators is to create division in our nation.' While making this accusation, they also gave the impression that some People of the Book and foreigners were also included in this conspiracy, so that they could successfully incite the national emotions of the Arabs against the Qur'an and the Prophet (pbuh).

The reason for calling the Prophet (pbuh) mad has passed in its proper place—stating that the Quraysh felt: first, the fear of the punishment of the Hereafter was so dominant over your heart that you continuously reminded of it; second, your tone possessed such certainty that everyone was influenced to think: if this person had not been appointed by Allah, why would he ruin his sleep and food for the sake of others? To remove this influence, the Quraysh began to say: 'Just as some people are afflicted with a mania regarding some things, and they continuously repeat it and see it everywhere, similarly, this person is also afflicted with the mania of the punishment. He sees it coming from every corner. There is no reality behind it; rather, this is mere madness. From where and why would punishment come upon us?'

"Indeed, We will remove the punishment a little; indeed, you will return [to disbelief]." (15)

Those Who are Slaves of Desires Make Temporary Repentance

That is, it is possible that upon your request—that if the punishment is removed from us, We will believe—We remove the punishment for a time, but you will return to the same way on which you walked before the punishment. The condition of the slaves of desires is such: when some trial is faced, they repent with great humility, but once the trial has passed, they depart as if no event had occurred, nor had they made any covenant, nor would they face any such thing in the future.

"The Day We will assault [you] with the greatest assault; indeed, We will take retribution." (16)

That is, if you escape from the grasp in this world, this should not be a cause of satisfaction. Remember that Day when We will seize with the greatest grasp. By 'the greatest assault' (Al-Batshat-al-Kubra), the grasp of the Resurrection is meant. The grasp of nations in this world is, after all, small compared to the Resurrection. The grasp of the Resurrection will be eternal, and on that Day, Allah Almighty will take full retribution from all criminals.

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 17–33

Next, the historical proof is presented for that which was described in the preceding passage. To warn the Quraysh, the story of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and Pharaoh is described briefly, from which the reality stands manifest: the plot which Pharaoh made against the messenger of God is the very same plot which the pharaohs of the Quraysh are making against the messenger of God. Allah punished the Egyptians for their plots, and all their pride was destroyed. In the same manner, these people will also fall under the grasp of God if they do not change their way... Recite the verses.

'And We certainly tried before them the people of Pharaoh, and there came to them a noble messenger,' (17)

Great Resemblance between the States of the Quraysh and the People of Pharaoh

The Qur'an has pointed in various places to the resemblance between the states of the Quraysh and the people of Pharaoh; here too, this is pointed to. The meaning is: just as We have placed the Quraysh in a trial today, We had placed the people of Pharaoh in a similar trial before. Just as these have received an abundance of comfort and prosperity, they had also received an abundance of wealth and power. Then, just as a noble messenger came to them for warning and the final establishment of proof, a noble messenger came to these people. By referring to this resemblance, the objective is to show that the end faced by them will also be faced by these people if they adopt their way.

This clarified the reality: the wealth and power which nations receive in this world is for trial. Allah, by bestowing all this, tests: upon receiving these blessings of Allah, do nations walk on the path of gratitude and worship, or adopt the path of rebellion and tyranny? If they choose this second path, they receive a specified term of respite, after which Allah Almighty wipes out their name.

Honor is the Specific Characteristic of the Office of Messengership

By describing the messenger with 'noble' (kareem), it is meant to show the reality: since the messenger comes as the ambassador of the King of the universe, honor is a necessary requirement of his office; no question arises whether he is rich or poor, nor whether he belongs to the royal family of Pharaoh or the poor family of the Children of Israel whom Pharaoh considered enslaved and disgraced. His primary attribute is that he is the ambassador of God, and who is the ambassador of God, no one is more honored than him except God Himself.

"[Saying], 'Deliver to me the servants of Allah. Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger,'" (18)

No One Can Make God's Servants Their Slaves

Before 'an' (that), a letter is omitted—meaning: the noble messenger came to them with this message: 'Deliver to me the servants of Allah.' This is that very same point which has passed in verse 47 of Surah Ta-Ha with the words: 'so send with us the Children of Israel'. However, in this phrase, Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) has also summarized the proof of his demand. The meaning is: those who are the servants of Allah, no one has the right to make them their slaves; therefore, let them go with me so that we perform the worship of our Lord as we please, without any hindrance. The question of where Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) wanted to take the Children of Israel has been discussed. There is no need to repeat.

An Implicit Threat

'Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger'—this assured Pharaoh and his chiefs: 'I am no claimant or fabricator; rather, in reality, I am His messenger. With complete honesty and trustworthiness, I am delivering the message which He has sent me to deliver to you.' Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) said this not merely to clarify his position, but it also contains an implicit threat: if I am declared a fabricator and denied, its consequences will be fatal. He Who has sent me as His messenger will take retribution from those who deny.

"And [saying], 'Do not exalt yourselves against Allah. Indeed, I bring you a clear authority.'" (19)

Rebelling against the Messenger is Rebelling against God

This is a part of the message: 'I have also been instructed to deliver this message: accept this command of God directly. If you show rebellion, this rebellion will not be against me alone, but primarily against God, because my primary status is that of His ambassador.'

'Indeed, I bring you a clear authority' (Inni ateekum bi-sultanin mubeen)—'sultan mubeen' refers to the miracles of the staff and the white hand. That is: 'I possess highly clear credentials of being a divine ambassador, and I show them to you so that no doubt remains in your minds regarding me.' Since Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) was sent as a messenger to a highly rebellious and tyrant king, Allah Almighty armed him from the beginning with such miracles as would establish proof against his opponents.

"And indeed, I seek refuge in my Lord and your Lord, lest you stone me." (20)

A Highly Eloquent Call and Effective Warning

Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) said this when his demand created a panic among the Egyptians. At that time, they threatened to kill him, and the danger arose that this crazy nation would stone you in panic. At that time, he addressed them: 'If you intend to stone me, I seek refuge in my Lord Who is my Lord and your Lord.' Within 'and your Lord' (wa Rabbikum), there is an effective appeal, a highly eloquent call, and a highly dignified warning, and also a subtle satire: 'Do not commit, at the word of this devil who has set himself up as your supreme lord, any action which will completely destroy your entire nation.'

"And if you do not believe me, then keep away from me." (21)

That is: 'If you are not ready to believe that I am the ambassador of God, at least do not commit the crime of killing or stoning me; rather, leave my way. Let me go wherever I want to take the Children of Israel. It was better for you to accept my word and believe. This would be the guarantee of your success in both this world and the Hereafter. If this is not acceptable, at least do not try to place obstacles in my way.'

"Then he called to his Lord, 'Indeed, these are a people [who are] criminals.'" (22)

Hazrat Musa's Supplication to His Lord

This appeal and warning of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) had no effect upon the Egyptians; rather, they persisted on their conspiracies and activities. Ultimately, Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) supplicated to his Lord: 'O Lord, these people are not ready to hear and accept; they are confirmed criminals; so You alone are Who can save us from their clutches.'

"[Allah said], 'Then travel with My servants by night. Indeed, you will be pursued.'" (23)

The Supplication Was Heard Immediately

This supplication was heard immediately; because of this, to show the immediate acceptance, there was no need even to bring any word showing that in response, Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) was instructed. The command was: 'Depart with My servants by night, and also remember that you will be pursued.' This warning was required because although Pharaoh, being troubled by those calamities which were sent upon Egypt in accordance with the supplications of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), permitted Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) to depart for a few days, when Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) departed with his entire nation, his intention changed. He realized that in giving permission, he had committed a mistake. Thus, he immediately pursued them with his armies and those of his chiefs to force them to return. Allah Almighty informed Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) of this situation beforehand so that no unnecessary delay occurred in their departure, but they tried to cross the sea in accordance with the specified program.

"And leave the sea calm. Indeed, they are an army to be drowned." (24)

Instruction to Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him)

'Rahwan' means calm. That is: 'Depart from the sea before the wind which kept the water away to clear the way for you ceases. As soon as you depart, the sea will become calm, and the water will return to its place. During this, the Egyptians pursuing you will be in the middle of the sea, and the water will envelope them in such a way that they will neither be able to turn backward nor proceed forward.'

"How much they left of gardens and springs And crops and noble places And comfort wherein they were amused." (25-27)

Having No Fear of God Is the Precursor of Destruction

That is, they were completely deprived of those gardens, springs, crops, and magnificent houses in which they showed arrogance, to become the food of the sea. They assumed that these things were the proof of their success and progress, and that those who warned them of their destruction were foolish. But it was proven: having no fear of God makes these things the precursor of destruction, and when destruction comes, none of these things is of any use.

"Thus. And We caused to inherit it another people." (28)

The Sunnah of Allah regarding the Criminals

After 'Thus' (Kadhalika), the words 'We deal with the criminals' are omitted. That is: 'Thus We have dealt with the criminals, and thus We will deal in the future.' 'And We caused to inherit it another people'—by 'another people', the Children of Israel are not meant here. After departing from Egypt, the return of the Children of Israel to Egypt is not proven. Perhaps this refers to those adjacent nations of whom the Egyptians always feared that they would join the Children of Israel to capture the country; from the Torah, it is learned that the policy of killing the male children of the Children of Israel was adopted under this fear.

"And the heaven and earth wept not for them, nor were they reprieved." (29)

That is: as long as they remained in authority, they assumed that they were the founders of a magnificent civilization and the entire world would remain subordinate to them; but upon their destruction, neither did the sky weep, nor did the earth shed tears, but everyone took a breath of relief.

It should be kept in mind here: upon the death of an oppressed person, even the sky is grieved and the earth cries out. In the Torah, it is written: 'The Lord said, "The voice of your brother's blood is crying to me from the ground."' But upon the destruction of the wrongdoers and disobedient, the heavens and the earth both rejoice. Particularly, upon the destruction of those against whom proof was fully established by His messenger.

"And We certainly saved the Children of Israel from the humiliating punishment, From Pharaoh. Indeed, he was haughty, of the transgressors." (30-31)

'From Pharaoh' (min Fir'awna) serves as an appositive (badal) to 'the humiliating punishment' (Al-'Adhabi-l-Muheen)—as if Allah Himself declared Pharaoh to be a humiliating punishment. Pharaoh considered Hazrat Musa and his people disgraceful, but in the sight of Allah Almighty, Pharaoh himself was not only disgraceful, but also a humiliating punishment.

'Indeed, he was haughty, of the transgressors'—this describes the cause of his being declared a humiliating punishment: because he was highly rebellious and crossed those limits which Allah appointed. Those who show rebellion before Allah are a punishment for the oppressed, and disgraceful with Allah.

"And We certainly chose them on the basis of knowledge over the worlds." (32)

The Nation Which Bears the Shariah is Worthy of Guidance

That is, We drowned Pharaoh and his people in the sea, and chose the Children of Israel, who were being trampled under their feet, for the guidance of the world. It should be clear: the nation which is made the bearer of the Shariah is naturally worthy and deserving of guiding the creation. This status is conditional upon their performing their duty with complete honesty; if they do not, Allah Almighty strips them of this status to entrust it to others. For a specific period, the Children of Israel held this status, but when they proved unworthy of it, they were deposed, and the duty of guiding the creation was entrusted to the Muslim Ummah.

A Philosophy of History

With 'on the basis of knowledge' ('ala 'ilmin), the philosophy of history is pointed to—showing: in this world, the removal and establishment of nations do not occur as accidents; rather, Allah Almighty, after testing them on His touchstone, chooses the nation He finds worthy, and rejects the nation He finds unworthy. From this, the lesson is learned: those who are deposed should beat their own heads over their incompetence, instead of blaming others, and those who receive authority should remain grateful to God, instead of falling into pride, and perform their duties. The entire plan of success and decline in this world is structured by Allah Almighty, and its foundation is on the character of nations. The knowledge of Allah is perfect, and His wisdom is impartial.

A Highly Important Warning to the Children of Israel

Within these verses, there is a highly important warning to those Children of Israel who, in the period of these verses' sending down, were active in opposing the Qur'an and the Prophet (pbuh). If they had understood a single point—that their choice was based on His knowledge, and now the decision of His knowledge is that they are no longer worthy of it—they would have escaped that bad end which they faced as a result of opposing Islam.

"And We gave them of signs that in which was a clear trial." (33)

The Trial of the Children of Israel through Favors

'Bal'a'' primarily means trial, but trial can be through favors and also through calamities. The trial of favor is to test gratitude, and of calamity to test patience. Here, the context is proof that it is used in the sense of favor and bounty, just as in verse 17 of Surah Al-Anfal, 'bala'' has occurred with the adjective 'hasanan'. This points to those favors which Allah Almighty bestowed upon the Children of Israel in various forms while crossing the sea, in their life in the wilderness, and during the conquest of Palestine, whose details have passed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 34–57

In the subsequent verses, the primary cause of the Quraysh's rebellion is pointed to—showing that these people do not possess any concept of life after this worldly life; because of this, they are lost in the present and carefree of the future. To remove this misconception, first, the rational and natural proofs of reward and punishment are described. After this, the fate of both the disbelievers and the believers is depicted. Recite the verses.

'Indeed, these people say, "It is not but our first death, and we will not be resurrected."' (34-35)

The Polytheists' Claim

That is, these people claim with great confidence: 'This warning of the Resurrection is merely an empty threat. This death which is faced in the world is the first death and also the last. After this, there is neither death nor life.'

"'Then bring [back] our forefathers, if you should be truthful.'" (36)

The Sole Baseless Proof of the Polytheists

In support of their claim, they demanded of the believers: 'If there is life after death, we will believe your truthfulness when you raise alive one of our deceased ancestors.' This same theme is expressed in Surah Al-Jathiyah as follows: 'And when Our clear verses are recited to them, their argument is only that they say, "Bring [back] our forefathers, if you should be truthful."' (25). The Qur'an has declared this to be their sole argument, and after referring to it, has ignored it without making any comment on it. This points to the fact: their sole argument, of which they are highly proud, is such that its absurdity is entirely obvious, needing no comment.

"Are they better or the people of Tubba' and those before them? We destroyed them; indeed, they were criminals." (37)

'The people of Tubba'' refers to the rulers of Yemen, whose power, glory, and intelligence were famous among the Arabs. The Arab poets mention their greatness extensively.

When God's Punishment Seizes Nations, They Commit Suicide with Their Own Weapons

In reply to their demand, the Qur'an asked the Quraysh: 'Are they superior in terms of material glory and intelligence, or were the rulers of Yemen superior? If they were superior, and despite this superiority God destroyed them—neither did their material power save them nor did their intelligence benefit them—on what basis of strength are they in the pride that no one can shake them? When God broke the necks of those stronger than them, why can He not break their necks?' The same was the case with previous nations. 'Aad, Thamud, and other nations were superior to them in every aspect; but when they rebelled, God wiped out their names; so what special distinction do they possess to remain favored of God even if they exceed them in rebellion? In the scale of God, value belongs to the character of nations, not to their material wealth and magnificent buildings, nor to their missiles and atomic bombs. When the character of nations becomes corrupt, He throws their very bombs, which they make to destroy others, over their own heads, and they are forced to commit suicide with their own weapons.

Punishing Nations in the World Is Proof of the Resurrection

There are so many examples of His law of retribution in this world that counting them is impossible; so why should anyone doubt that Day of Recompense in which the Creator of the universe will reward or punish individuals as He rewarded or punished nations in this world.

"And We did not create the heavens and the earth and that between them in play. We did not create them except in truth, but most of them do not know." (38-39)

The Hereafter Is the Necessary Requirement of His Attributes

Above, proof for reward and punishment was presented from history and the signs of the horizons. Now, proof is presented from the attributes of God and their moral requirements. It states: We have not created the heavens and the earth as a plaything, but with truth and purpose. The requirement of this purpose is that this world ends one day, and after it, a Day comes in which its Creator rewards those who led a life in accordance with His pleasure, and punishes those who disobeyed. If no such Day comes, and this world continues as it is, or ends in vain one day—neither the righteous receiving any reward nor the wicked any punishment—it means its Creator is, (God forbid), a player who is sitting in the heavens observing the play of tyranny, and when his desire is filled, will break it. Obviously, this is such a bad thought regarding the Wise and Merciful Creator as cannot be conceived, but this is a strange thing that the majority of people are caught in this doubt and are entirely unaware of its consequences.

We have discussed this in detail in the previous Surahs, so we limit our discussion to an allusion here.

"Indeed, the Day of Judgement is the appointed time for them all -" (40)

The Logical Requirement of This World's Being with Truth

This is the logical consequence of this world's being with truth—showing: it is contrary to the knowledge, justice, wisdom, and mercy of God that this world continues as it is or ends in vain; rather, it is necessary that a Day comes when He sits on the seat of justice and decides everyone's affairs. Those who led a life of truth and uprightness receive the reward, and those who committed tyranny face the punishment, and that Day will be the Day of the manifestation of perfect justice, such that no one can escape from it. Small and great, leaders and followers, kings and beggars—the justice of all will be made, and made in such a way that no reality remains hidden, nor can anyone obstruct His justice. Keep particularly in view the word 'all' (ajma'een).

"The Day when no protector will avail a protector [i.e., relative] at all, nor will they be helped," (41)

'Mawla' is used for those family and tribe members with whom a person has relationship of blood and lineage, and with whom there is the feeling of mutual support. It states: that Day will be of such self-concern that no close relative will be of any use to anyone. 'nor will they be helped'—and they will not be helped from any other side either. The context indicates that this is the negation of that help which the polytheists expected of their assumed partners.

"Except those [upon] whom Allah has mercy. Indeed, He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful." (42)

Those Deserving of Allah's Mercy

That is, on that Day, success will be the share only of those upon whom He shows His favor, and He will show His favor only to those who prove deserving of it. He is both 'Al-Aziz' (Exalted in Might) and 'Al-Raheem' (Merciful). Whomever He seizes, no one can save; and whom He finds deserving of His mercy, no one can deprive. The meaning is: the servants must fear Him alone, and hope for mercy from Him alone.

"Indeed, the tree of zaqqum Is food for the sinner. Like murky oil, it boils within bellies Like the boiling of hot water." (43-46)

The Fate of the Criminals

Following the mention of the Day of Judgment, this is the depiction of the fate of the sinners. It states: the tree of Zaqqum will be the food of the sinners. This Zaqqum is the Zaqqum of Hell; therefore, its true reality is known only to Allah. From the Zaqqum of this world, we can only form a general idea of it, but cannot understand its actual reality.

Different meanings have been explained for 'Muhl'. Keeping the etymology in view, I have preferred the meaning of murky oil (the dregs of oil)—stating: this Zaqqum will enter their bellies to boil like the dregs of oil, and will boil like water.

Meaning, its boiling will be highly offensive and like the dregs of oil in terms of heat, burn, and bitterness, and like water in terms of boiling. When oil is boiled, the heat is severe but it does not boil over; when water is boiled, it boils over. Here, both characteristics of boiling have been combined.

"[It will be commanded], 'Seize him and drag him into the midst of the Hellfire, Then pour over his head from the punishment of boiling water.'" (47-48)

Some words are omitted here due to contextual clarity—implying: a command will be issued regarding them from the divine court; instead, the command itself is referred to: 'Seize them and drag them into the middle of Hell, and pour boiling water over their heads.'

"[It will be said], 'Taste! Indeed, you are the [self-proclaimed] honored, the noble! Indeed, this is what you used to dispute.'" (49-50)

This can be said through the language of state of the punishment, or through the tongue of those who punish. They will be told: 'In the world, you were highly powerful and honored, and in this pride, you denied this Day. Today, taste its flavor. This is the very same thing regarding which you disputed.'

"Indeed, the righteous will be in a secure place, In gardens and springs," (51-52)

The Reward of the Pious

Following the fate of the sinners, this is the depiction of the reward of the pious—stating that they will be in a secure place, where neither will they have any fear nor any grief; they will be in gardens and springs, and will have no fear that anyone can deprive them of these blessings, or that any decline will come upon them, or that they will be questioned regarding these blessings.

"Wearing [garments] of fine silk and heavy brocade, facing each other." (53)

'Sundus' (fine silk) and 'Istabraq' (heavy brocade) are the names of silken garments. Some have distinguished between them as thin and thick. But since this is the mention of the silken garments of Paradise, this discussion is unnecessary. Their true reality is known only to Allah Almighty.

'Mutaqabileen' (facing each other) means sitting facing one another. In terms of construction, a verb like 'they will sit' must be assumed—meaning: 'wearing garments of fine silk and heavy brocade, they will sit facing one another.' Sitting facing one another is proof of mutual love and trust. Since they advised one another in the path of truth in the world, and its magnificent end will be before them, they will sit with joy. On the contrary, the disbelievers and their leaders will curse one another.

"Thus. And We will marry them to fair women with large, [beautiful] eyes. They will call therein for every [kind of] fruit, secure." (54-55)

It states: for the completion of their joy, We will marry them to Houris with large, beautiful eyes. In Arabic literature, this attribute is a comprehensive expression of a woman's perfect beauty.

'They will call...'—meaning they will have an abundance of all kinds of fruits; they will ask for whatever fruit they desire from the servants. Neither will they fear any shortage of fruits, nor any deprivation from this comfort, nor any fear of death. Secure from every danger, they will enjoy eternal comfort.

"They will not taste death therein except the first death, and He will have protected them from the punishment of Hellfire [As] bounty from your Lord. That is the great attainment." (56-57)

That is, they will never taste death again after that first death they tasted in the world. Their life will be eternal, as will their comfort. They achieved release from death and their Lord protected them from Hell... this is a special favor which your Lord bestowed upon them, and this indeed is the greatest success, not that on which the lovers of the world are infatuated, being lost in its love to think that this worldly life is everything, after which there is no life and no death.

6. Conclusion of the Surah: Verses 58-59

It will be recalled that this Surah began with the description of the Qur'an's greatness—stating that it is a highly great blessing with which Allah Almighty has blessed the Arabs. If they value it, they will inherit a great success in both this world and the Hereafter, and if in their pride they disregard it, they should remember that it will become a highly great punishment for them, and they will prepare their own destruction in both this world and the Hereafter. After this introductory theme, the proofs of the Qur'an's claims were described. Now at the end, from a new aspect, He reminded of that very theme with which the Surah began. Thus, the Surah ended on the same theme with which it began. This style has passed in the previous Surahs. Highly eloquent examples of this are found in the speeches of high-class speakers. The speaker, after introducing his speech with some theme, ends on its reminder so that the primary point is refreshed in the minds of the audience.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"And We have only made it [i.e., the Qur'an] easy in your tongue that perhaps they will be reminded." (58)

'Fa' (And) shows the connection of the subsequent with the preceding theme, and the connection is what we pointed to.

The Meaning of the Qur'an's Being Made Easy

'Tayseer' (making easy) means making something suitable, compatible, and robust for some purpose. 'Yassara-l-farsa lil-rukoob' means: he equipped the horse with saddle, stirrup, rein, and other accessories to make it ready for riding. Similarly, 'We made the Qur'an easy for the reminder' (yassarna-l-Qur'ana lidh-dhikr) means We made the Qur'an highly suitable, robust, and equipped with all necessary accessories for the purpose of warning and reminding. Those who have interpreted its meaning as: 'the Qur'an is a highly simple and plain book' have not understood the actual reality of this word. There is no doubt that the Qur'an is a highly easy Book, but this ease is in terms of that high objective of warning and reminding for which He sent it down. This objective contains various aspects, and since the Qur'an is comprehensive of all its aspects and their necessary accessories, it contains highly deep and difficult aspects, though to make these difficult aspects clear, the methods the Qur'an has adopted are such as no one else can adopt. However, these things are in themselves in need of deep reflection, because they relate to wisdom, and wisdom requires deep reflection. We have pointed to some of its aspects under verse 97 of Surah Maryam, and further light will, God willing, follow in the commentary of Surah Al-Qamar.

'in your tongue' (bi-lisanika) refers to the standard language of the Quraysh, which was the highest example of eloquent Arabic, and of whose eloquence the Prophet (pbuh) was the perfect manifestation. This points to a highly important aspect of the Qur'an's being made easy—showing: to establish proof against the Quraysh, We sent down this Book in your language, which is the highest example of their language. If they do not understand it even in this language, the fault lies neither in the Book nor in you, but in the corruption of their own hearts, for which you are not responsible.

'that perhaps they will be reminded'—this describes the objective of sending down the Qur'an with this care: that they receive reminding, i.e., remember what is imbued within their nature, take lesson from the signs of the horizons and ourselves, and remember that teaching which came through previous prophets, particularly their ancestor Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him). The Qur'an is reminding of all these matters in the best of languages and the best of styles. Within this clause is also a warning: if they are not ready to receive this reminding, then let them prepare for that end which is destined for such rebellious people, and of which their own past history is warning them.

"So watch; indeed, they [too] are watching." (59)

Within this verse, the warning implied above stands manifest. The meaning is: if these people are not ready to benefit from this Book, and are persistent that they will believe only after seeing the punishment of which it is warning them, then you also await that punishment. Now, the decision depends on this. Have a look at verse 10 above.

By the grace of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed on these lines. Praise be to Allah for His favor.

Rahman Abad
22 May, 1976 AD
22 Jumada-th-Thani, 1396 AH.
